summary: Cathy O'Neil recounts Jeff Larson's visit to the Lede Program at Columbia, where he demonstrated impressive data journalism projects from ProPublica. Larson showed how they reverse-engineered Obama's political targeting algorithm using decision trees, applied clustering algorithms to sort foreign language emails, and bypassed captcha systems using neural networks he built over a lunch break. The students then toured ProPublica's offices and met other data journalists working on secret projects. O'Neil emphasizes how inspiring it was that most of these skilled data journalists, including Larson himself, lacked formal computer science training.
key_points: ProPublica crowdsourced and reverse-engineered Obama's political targeting algorithm, discovering it used decision trees
Clustering algorithms like cosine similarity and k-means can be used to find interesting content in foreign language email datasets
Neural networks can bypass captcha systems about 90% of the time
Most data journalists at ProPublica lack formal CS training - Larson was a literature major
Hands-on demonstrations helped students realize they already had applicable skills
money_quotes: They first crowdsourced, then reverse engineered Obama's political targeting algorithm.
Since it was an awesome project important to democracy, we also got to talk about democracy.
For a moment the class was like, 'hey we can do this!' and it was true.
He seemed to somehow remain humble whilst explaining that he did this over a lunch break.
Only one of them had formal CS training (Jeff was a lit major!).
key_insight: Data journalism that holds power accountable doesn't require formal computer science credentials—curiosity, persistence, and willingness to learn technical skills can take you remarkably far.
